description: Recommended next moves for each MEDDPICC factor when scored yellow or red

yellow.diagnosis: Numbers exist but are seller-generated, not prospect-validated
moves: Ask the champion to confirm or correct your estimates in the next call
Build a simple ROI model and walk through it live -- let them edit the inputs
Reference a comparable customer's published metrics as an anchor point
anti_patterns: Sending a PDF ROI calculator and hoping they fill it out
Quoting industry averages without connecting to their specific situation

moves: In the next call, ask: 'What does this problem cost you today in dollars, hours, or risk?'
Propose a discovery question that forces quantification: volume, frequency, cost per incident
If they can't quantify, ask what would happen if they did nothing for 12 months
anti_patterns: Skipping metrics because the champion says they 'just know' it's a problem
Accepting vague statements like 'it's costing us a lot' as quantification

yellow.diagnosis: EB identified but has not engaged directly with your team
moves: Ask the champion to schedule a 20-minute intro with the EB, framed around business outcomes not product
Create a one-page executive summary tailored to the EB's priorities
Offer to brief the EB on industry trends relevant to their role -- give before you ask
anti_patterns: Going around the champion to reach the EB directly
Asking for an EB meeting without giving the champion a reason the EB would say yes

red.diagnosis: Economic Buyer is unknown or inaccessible
moves: Ask the champion directly: 'Who signs off on a purchase of this size?'
Research the org chart via LinkedIn, annual reports, or press releases
If the champion deflects, test: 'If you and I agreed this was the right solution today, what would happen next?'
anti_patterns: Assuming the champion is the EB without confirming
Proceeding to proposal without knowing who approves budget

yellow.diagnosis: Criteria inferred from context but never explicitly confirmed
moves: In the next call, ask: 'What are the three things this solution must do to get a yes from your team?'
Share your understanding of their criteria and ask them to correct or add to it
Ask how they evaluated the last vendor they bought -- what mattered most?
anti_patterns: Assuming technical fit equals decision criteria
Letting a demo substitute for criteria discovery

moves: Dedicate the next meeting to understanding evaluation criteria before demoing anything
Ask: 'If you had to write the internal business case, what would need to be true?'
Propose a structured evaluation framework and let them react to it
anti_patterns: Running a demo without knowing what they're evaluating
Pitching features and hoping something sticks

yellow.diagnosis: General shape of the process is known but steps are not mapped
moves: Map the process together: 'Let's walk through what happens between your team saying yes and a signed contract'
Ask who else needs to weigh in and at what stage
Propose a mutual action plan with dates and share it for feedback
anti_patterns: Assuming their process mirrors your sales process
Skipping the process map because the champion says 'it should be straightforward'

moves: Ask: 'Walk me through how your company bought the last piece of software in this price range'
Ask the champion who else has been involved in similar decisions
If early stage, flag this as a required output of the next meeting
anti_patterns: Moving to proposal without understanding the approval chain
Treating urgency as a substitute for process clarity

yellow.diagnosis: General awareness that procurement exists but steps and timeline are unmapped
moves: Ask: 'Once we have internal alignment, what does procurement look like? Legal review? Security?'
Ask the champion for the name of the procurement contact and typical cycle time
Offer to send a pre-filled security questionnaire or compliance docs proactively
anti_patterns: Waiting until after verbal agreement to discover a 90-day procurement cycle
Assuming the champion can bypass procurement

red.diagnosis: Paper process has not been discussed at all
moves: Raise it now: 'I want to make sure we're not surprised by timeline. Can you walk me through what happens after your team says go?'
Ask if there's a security review, legal review, or procurement board involved
Ask for any vendor onboarding forms or requirements early
anti_patterns: Discovering legal requirements the week you expect to close
Never asking about procurement because it feels premature

yellow.diagnosis: Pain identified by seller and acknowledged by prospect, but not articulated in their own words
moves: Ask the champion to describe the problem as if explaining it to their CEO
Ask: 'What happens to your team if this problem isn't solved in the next 6 months?'
Connect the pain to a personal consequence -- career risk, team burden, missed targets
anti_patterns: Telling them their pain instead of having them tell you
Accepting 'yeah, that's a problem' as deep pain confirmation

red.diagnosis: Pain is assumed, not confirmed by the prospect
moves: Go back to discovery: 'Help me understand -- what triggered you to look at this now?'
Ask: 'What have you tried before to solve this? What happened?'
If they can't articulate pain, this may not be a real opportunity -- qualify harder
anti_patterns: Projecting pain from similar companies onto this prospect
Moving past discovery because a meeting was booked

yellow.diagnosis: Champion has 2-3 of the 4 criteria: motive, influence, EB access, active internal selling
moves: Identify which criterion is missing and address it directly
If missing internal selling: arm the champion with a one-page business case they can share
If missing EB access: ask who else in the org could make an introduction
If missing influence: consider whether there's a stronger champion candidate
anti_patterns: Treating a friendly contact as a champion without testing their willingness to sell internally
Assuming motive equals influence

red.diagnosis: No champion exists or current contact meets 0-1 criteria
moves: Test the current contact: 'Would you be willing to introduce me to [decision maker]?'
If they decline or deflect, search for a different champion with more at stake
Ask: 'Who in your organization cares most about solving this problem?'
Consider multi-threading to find someone with real motive and influence
anti_patterns: Calling someone a champion because they respond to emails
Building the entire deal on a single contact who has no power

yellow.diagnosis: Competitors identified but position relative to them is unclear
moves: Ask: 'How do you see us compared to [competitor]? What do they do better? What do we do better?'
Share a differentiation point and ask the champion to validate it
Ask if the competitor has done a demo or submitted a proposal
anti_patterns: Trash-talking competitors instead of differentiating on value
Ignoring competition because the champion seems positive about you

moves: Ask directly: 'Are you evaluating other solutions? Who else is in the conversation?'
Ask: 'What would you do if you decided not to go with us?' -- surfaces alternatives including do-nothing
Research the prospect's tech stack and recent vendor announcements for clues
anti_patterns: Assuming you're the only option because they haven't mentioned anyone else
Never asking about competition because it feels awkward
